title: From Me to You: Shimamura's Letter
description: Shimamura writes back to Adachi—sharing memories of first impressions, the beauty of their shared life, and the quiet joy of loving someone.
content:
Dear Adachi Sakura,

Skipping the usual formalities, and all that…

This is the first time I've ever written you a letter. I've sent postcards before, but this might be the first time I've written something proper. I'm using the polite desu/masu style simply because it feels comfortable for me—there's no deeper meaning behind it, so please don't worry.

I thought it would be easier to express our feelings if we each wrote a letter to the other, so I suggested it on a whim. But now that I'm actually writing, I realize I don't have much to say. Or rather, I've realized I'm surprisingly bad at putting things into words. Sometimes I regret not paying more attention during high school.

I remember the first time I saw you—how hot the day was, and the sound of cicadas ringing in my ears. I can almost recreate the moment perfectly in my mind. When you sat near me, I often found myself looking at your profile. It was the first time I'd ever looked at a classmate so closely, so maybe I have nothing to compare it to—but to me, you looked more beautiful than anyone else.

You always seemed expressionless at first glance, but when we talked about the most trivial things, your eyes would flicker—like emotions rippling just beneath the surface of water. Thinking back, I might have gone up to the second floor of the gym just to see your face, your profile, your expressions.

I never imagined that after so many years, the two of us would end up living together—choosing a home of our own—in a country just for the two of us. My next goal is to build a castle in that country. That's why I've been working so hard lately. It may never happen, but having a goal makes life easier.

What kind of things do you want us to do together, Adachi? Lately, that's what I find myself thinking about as I lie in bed with my eyes closed. I imagine all sorts of possibilities—'that would be nice,' 'this sounds fun'—and I end up getting excited all by myself. I think that's kind of amazing, actually.

I'm well aware that I'm a lazy person by nature. And yet, here I am, earnestly peering at dreams lined up like items in a display case... It feels incredible. Being with you has made me truly understand what it means for us to share a life.

You always give me energy. I feel happy coming home from work and finding you waiting for me—and just as happy when I get home first and wait for you.

I'm not good with fancy words, but I'm grateful every single day. It's embarrassing to say it face to face, but in a letter, somehow it feels easier—though still a little embarrassing.

We're supposed to exchange our letters when we finish, but I don't want you to see my face while you're reading mine. So I'm going to run off the moment I give it to you. I'll pick up a few things while I'm out, so please wait for me until I manage to calm down.

Even though this letter is short, writing it feels strangely satisfying. Maybe it wouldn't be so bad to write each other letters like this once in a while. It's a little embarrassing knowing they'll remain as something tangible—but that very permanence makes it feel meaningful, too.

Let's keep taking good care of each other so we can stay healthy, and I hope we can be together for as long as possible—even if it's just one more day.

See you soon—very soon.

And since we're writing these letters in neighboring rooms, I think it's best not to write while reading aloud.

I love you, Adachi.
